author: @simongross3122
text:
I used to own some eyeglasses that had a liquid lens that deformed using a little slider above the frame.  They actually had two sets of lenses.  The outer lenses were my long-distance prescription and were just normal lenses.  The inner lenses were these liquid thingies and could adjust short-distance vision quite effectively.  They were very good until they developed a leak.  I bought replacements (very expensive) and then the new ones leaked while still under warranty.  The company went belly-up and so far, I've not found equivalents.  

I keep looking, but I've not seen any product that works credibly.  The idea is sound though.  I wish someone would produce some good adjustable glasses.  They are far more comfortable than multifocals.

author: @na9rz
text: If I remember my high school physics properly, sticking a liquid with a higher refractive index in there should give you more magnification. It happens that glycerol, or glycerine, has a higher refractive index than water, 1.46 and 1.33 respectively. While water is a lot easier to work with than glycerol, I think it's worth a shot.

text: Should be able to 3d print the frame :)

author: @SoundzAlive1
text: Hi Rob, I have seen a lens made with water to magnify the sun's rays to heat something or start a fire. Simply spread a sheet of plastic across a square frame (say a metre but size is irrelevant ) attach to frame (continuously around edge) then place horizontally and pour water on top. It will automatically stretch to make a perfect lens shape (convex I think) with a perfectly flat topside. Of course the larger the more powerful. André in Sydney

author: @timreynolds2264

author: @russellzauner
text:
Make a lens with clear, degassed, agar (I don't like using gelatin).  I haven't tried it but it should work.  If it works, then colors could be added.  If that works then we can make an array.  If that works we can make filters.  If that works then we can shrink the sizes of the array elements.  If that works, then if we overfill a silicone candy tray that makes tiny candies then we have an array on a substrate that also has optical properties.  I think I need to try this.

I once worked with a liquid lens that was adjusted using piezoelectric actuators; another one was voice coil operated.  It's very interesting stuff - one liquid lens tech was developed just to do deep sea imaging.

author: @sebastianstewart6894
text: Oldest magnifying glass.

author: @Berkana
text:
Robert, you can also make large spherical curvature mirrors for solar concentration by taking a large shallow circular cake pan, stretching out a sheet of mylar or foil, and gluing the foil to the rim of the cake pan, followed by pulling a mild vacuum in the cake pan. With this method you can actually make frighteningly powerful solar collectors.

(There are some videos on YouTube claiming that this makes a parabolic reflector, but this is not correct; the curvature is spherical, just like a bubble would be, since air pressure pushes perpendicularly on the membrane at all locations.)

author: @ProlificInvention
text: You reminded me of an I had an idea for making these clear large domes from liquid floor acrylic after I saw a video of a guy using the method to make submarine windows and depth testing them.  You would need a large inflatable ball or alternatively a dome shaped object which you prepare by using self adhesive foam around it for a border, secure so it can't move, then apply the acrylic.  I also looked up what large clear domes cost from manufacturers, they are quite expensive.
